data.title: Is It Time To Reassess Verisk Analytics (VRSK) After Recent Share Price Rebound?
data.description:
• If you are looking at Verisk Analytics and wondering whether the current share price gives you good value, this article walks through what the numbers are really saying about the stock.
  • Over the short term, the share price has moved around, with a 3.5% return over 7 days and 16.3% over 30 days, compared with a 2.8% decline year to date and a 28.0% decline over the past year. This is set against 3-year and 5-year returns of 21.5% and 28.9% respectively.
  • Recent coverage has focused on Verisk Analytics' role as a data and analytics provider to commercial services, with investors paying close attention to how its offerings are used across insurance and risk-related workflows. This context has helped shape how the market views the stock after the recent price moves, especially for investors who care about longer-term cash generation and balance sheet strength.
  • Simply Wall St currently scores Verisk Analytics at 2 out of 6 on its valuation checks. This raises the question of how different methods like DCF, multiples and market-based benchmarks compare, and whether there is an even richer way to think about value that we will come to at the end of this article.

Verisk Analytics scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Verisk Analytics Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes estimates of the cash a company could generate in the future and discounts those amounts back to today, to arrive at an estimate of what the business might be worth right now.

For Verisk Analytics, the model uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on cash flow projections. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about $1.19b. Analysts provide detailed estimates out to 2029, and Simply Wall St extends these with its own extrapolations through 2035. On these projections, free cash flow is expected to reach about $1.54b by 2030, with later years growing at modest quoted rates.

After discounting all those projected cash flows back to today, the DCF model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of $233.64 per share. Compared with the current share price, this implies the stock is about 8.0% undervalued, which is a relatively small gap.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

Verisk Analytics is fairly valued according to our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF), but this can change at a moment's notice. Track the value in your watchlist or portfolio and be alerted on when to act.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Verisk Analytics.

Approach 2: Verisk Analytics Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like Verisk Analytics, the P/E ratio is a useful shorthand for how much investors are paying for each dollar of current earnings. It links directly to what the business is earning today, which most investors find easier to relate to than more complex cash flow models.

What counts as a “normal” P/E often reflects what the market expects for future growth and how risky those earnings might be. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher uncertainty usually point to a lower one.

Verisk Analytics currently trades on a P/E of 32.63x. That sits above the Professional Services industry average of 21.31x, and slightly below the peer group average of 34.06x. Simply Wall St also calculates a proprietary “Fair Ratio” of 26.44x, which is the P/E it would expect for Verisk Analytics after factoring in elements like earnings growth, risk profile, profit margins, market cap and its industry.

This Fair Ratio is more tailored than a simple peer or industry comparison because it tries to match the multiple to the company’s own fundamentals instead of broad group averages. With the current P/E of 32.63x above the Fair Ratio of 26.44x, the stock screens as overvalued on this metric.

Result: OVERVALUED

Earlier we mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation. Let us introduce you to Narratives, which are simple stories you build around Verisk Analytics that link your view of its future revenue, earnings and margins to a forecast, then to a fair value, and ultimately to a clear comparison of Fair Value versus today’s price on Simply Wall St’s Community page. On that page, millions of investors share views that update automatically when fresh news or earnings arrive. data.title: Cirrus Logic Earnings Beat Fuels AI PC And Automotive Expansion Hopes
data.description:
• Cirrus Logic (NasdaqGS:CRUS) reported quarterly results that exceeded expectations, supported by demand for its smartphone components and a favorable product mix.
  • The company outlined plans to broaden its product portfolio into AI PCs, mainstream PCs, prosumer devices, and automotive applications.
  • These moves mark an effort to diversify beyond its core smartphone audio business into areas linked to AI and next generation computing and vehicles.

Cirrus Logic focuses on mixed signal audio and voice chips that sit inside many smartphones and other consumer devices. The latest quarter highlighted how important that smartphone demand still is for the business, even as the broader semiconductor industry continues to adjust to changing device cycles and AI related interest.

For you as an investor, a key consideration is how these AI PC and automotive efforts might influence Cirrus Logic's revenue mix over time. This article outlines what the company is doing in each new category so you can evaluate how these moves align with your own assessment of the stock's risk and opportunity profile.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Cirrus Logic by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Cirrus Logic.

See which insiders are buying and buying and selling Cirrus Logic following this latest news.

Cirrus Logic's latest quarter appears to have given investors more confidence in the business, with results coming in ahead of guidance and fresh detail on AI PC and automotive products. That combination of operational delivery and future product commentary often attracts both short-term traders and longer-term shareholders. The stronger earnings backdrop sits alongside an earnings consensus that has moved higher and several target price increases, which together point to a more constructive tone from the analyst community. For you, the key question is whether this momentum is mainly tied to current smartphone strength or whether AI PCs, mainstream PCs, prosumer devices, and auto can eventually become meaningful parts of the story.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Cirrus Logic Narrative

  • The push into AI PCs, laptops, and automotive lines up closely with the existing narrative that Cirrus Logic is trying to reduce its reliance on a small set of smartphone customers and broaden its mixed signal content across more devices.
  • At the same time, the narrative flags that non-smartphone segments are still early and uncertain, so investors may want to be careful about extrapolating one strong quarter into a smooth transition away from smartphone dependence.
  • The recent focus on investor reaction, including higher analyst targets and rating changes, adds a sentiment layer that the narrative does not fully capture, especially around how quickly expectations can reset if AI or auto ramps come in slower than hoped.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Cirrus Logic to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Analysts expect earnings to decline on average over the next few years, which suggests that even with new AI PC and automotive products, profit trends may not move in a straight line.
  • ⚠️ Cirrus Logic still has meaningful exposure to a small number of large smartphone customers, so any change in ordering patterns or supplier choices could have a noticeable impact on revenue and margins.
  • 🎁 Earnings grew by 32.3% over the past year, which gives management more room to keep funding R&D in areas like AI PCs and automotive haptics compared with some peers.
  • 🎁 The stock trades on a P/E that is below the broader US market, and is flagged as good value relative to peers in the semiconductor group that includes companies like Analog Devices and Texas Instruments.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, it is worth tracking how much of Cirrus Logic's future revenue still comes from flagship smartphones versus newer areas like AI PCs, prosumer hardware, and automotive haptics. Watch for design wins with large PC and auto customers, and how those translate into actual shipment volumes rather than just product announcements. Keep an eye on analyst estimates after this strong quarter too, because any revisions to earnings expectations or changes in ratings will show you how quickly sentiment is shifting relative to the company's execution.

data.title: Is Thomson Reuters (TSX:TRI) Still Fairly Priced After Recent Share Price Rebound?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Thomson Reuters at around US$151 per share is still a quality business at a fair price or starting to look stretched, you are not alone.
  • The stock has moved sharply in the short term, with returns of 15.3% over 7 days and 18.4% over 30 days, even though the return over 1 year sits at a 39.6% decline and the year to date figure is a 12.8% decline.

These themes help frame why sentiment around Thomson Reuters can shift quickly as the market weighs long term contract driven revenue against questions about pricing power and product relevance.

In the sections ahead we will walk through the main valuation approaches and then finish with a broader way to think about what that score really means for you.

Thomson Reuters scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Thomson Reuters Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model projects a company’s future cash flows and then discounts them back to today using a required rate of return. The result is an estimate of what the business may be worth based on the cash it is expected to generate for shareholders.

For Thomson Reuters, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on cash flow projections. The latest twelve month free cash flow sits at about $2.03b. Analyst and extrapolated estimates suggest free cash flow between $1.4b and $2.9b over the coming decade, with a specific projection of $1.54b in 2030. Simply Wall St only has direct analyst inputs for the nearer years, with later years extrapolated from those patterns.

Discounting these projected cash flows back to today produces an estimated intrinsic value of $103.04 per share. Compared with the current share price of around CA$151, the model indicates the stock is about 47.0% overvalued on this basis.

Result: OVERVALUED

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Thomson Reuters.

Approach 2: Thomson Reuters Price vs Earnings

For a profitable business like Thomson Reuters, the P/E ratio is a useful way to gauge what investors are paying for each dollar of earnings. It links directly to the bottom line, which is ultimately what supports dividends and buybacks over time.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends on how the market views a company’s growth potential and risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk often lines up with a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk tends to point to a lower one.

Thomson Reuters currently trades on a P/E of 33.54x. That sits above the Professional Services industry average of 16.48x but below the peer group average of 45.95x. To refine this comparison, Simply Wall St uses a proprietary “Fair Ratio”, which is the P/E level it would expect for the company given its earnings growth profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and risk factors. For Thomson Reuters, this Fair Ratio is 37.55x.

Because the Fair Ratio adjusts for company specific traits rather than relying only on broad averages, it can offer a more tailored reference point. Compared with this Fair Ratio, the current P/E of 33.54x suggests Thomson Reuters is somewhat undervalued on this metric.

data.title: SoftBank Deepens OpenAI Bet As AI Infrastructure Ambitions Reshape Risk Profile
data.description:
• SoftBank Group (TSE:9984) is repositioning itself as an AI native infrastructure provider, aiming to support distributed AI workloads across both edge and cloud.
  • The company is pursuing a multibillion dollar loan facility reportedly targeting up to US$40 billion, with a large portion intended to increase its exposure to OpenAI.
  • These moves come as SoftBank shifts further away from its roots as a traditional telecom carrier toward a broader AI focused platform model.

For you as an investor, the key point is that SoftBank is tying its strategy more closely to AI infrastructure and large scale model providers. The group already spans telecom, investments and technology platforms, so this AI focus fits into an existing ecosystem that includes connectivity, data centers and capital.

The plan to secure one of the larger recent financing deals to back OpenAI exposure adds both opportunity and concentration risk to the situation. If you are following TSE:9984, it is worth tracking how much of the balance sheet is committed to AI related assets and how these positions interact with its telecom and investment operations over time.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for SoftBank Group by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on SoftBank Group.

4 things going right for SoftBank Group that this headline doesn't cover.

SoftBank tying its telecom network, data centers and capital to an AI-native infrastructure push is a clear attempt to position itself closer to Nvidia, Microsoft or Alphabet, which already blend cloud, chips and AI platforms. The twist here is the scale and structure of the OpenAI exposure. A loan facility reportedly up to US$40b, on top of an existing stake of around 11%, concentrates a lot of financial and execution risk into one AI partner while SoftBank is also talking about open-sourcing parts of its AI-RAN technology and expanding a partner ecosystem. For you, the key question is whether this shifts SoftBank from being mostly an owner of AI assets to an operator of AI infrastructure where returns, timelines and capital needs can look different. The bridge-loan format, expected to be repaid in roughly 12 months, adds a timing element because PayPay’s delayed IPO and Vision Fund mark to market moves already influence the balance sheet. This is why many investors are watching leverage, interest coverage and loan-to-value ratios as closely as the AI story itself.

How This Fits Into The SoftBank Group Narrative

  • The push into AI-native infrastructure and heavier OpenAI exposure lines up with the narrative that future gains could be driven by AI assets and data infrastructure commitments.
  • The use of large, short tenor financing for an "all in" OpenAI bet may amplify the leverage and funding cost concerns already highlighted as potential headwinds in the narrative.
  • The focus on open-sourcing AI-RAN technology and expanding an ecosystem of partners looks under-represented in the existing narrative, which concentrates more on asset monetization and portfolio valuations.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for SoftBank Group to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Concentration into OpenAI through a loan facility reportedly up to US$40b increases exposure to a single AI name at the same time analysts have flagged that interest payments are not well covered by earnings.
  • ⚠️ SoftBank’s high share price volatility and existing leverage could magnify any setbacks if AI-related assets, IPOs or financing conditions turn out less favorable than expected.
  • 🎁 The AI-native infrastructure push, including AI-RAN and edge to cloud workloads, could help SoftBank plug into demand similar to what large peers like Microsoft and Alphabet are targeting through their AI and cloud offerings.
  • 🎁 Existing rewards flagged by analysts, such as revenue growth expectations and a P/E that is below the broader JP market, give some investors room to argue that the market has not fully priced in successful execution of the AI-focused plan.

What To Watch Going Forward

You may want to keep an eye on how quickly SoftBank converts the AI-native infrastructure vision into concrete commercial deals with carriers, enterprises and cloud providers, and whether these offset funding and concentration risks from the OpenAI exposure. Updates from the upcoming Morgan Stanley Technology, Media & Telecom Conference and the May 7, 2026 earnings release could provide more detail on leverage metrics, loan terms and how management plans to balance AI investments with asset sales such as any future PayPay IPO. For a company that moves this much capital, the interaction between portfolio marks, debt costs and new AI infrastructure commitments is likely to remain central to the thesis.

data.title: Chip Wilson Challenges Lululemon Governance As ShowZero Fabric Targets Growth
data.description:
• Lululemon athletica (NasdaqGS:LULU) founder Chip Wilson has launched an activist campaign, nominating three independent director candidates and calling for a board and governance overhaul.
  • The campaign pushes for board declassification and a sharper focus on creativity and brand vision at a time when the company is preparing for a new CEO.
  • In parallel, Lululemon has introduced ShowZero, a sweat-concealing fabric technology created with tennis player Frances Tiafoe and set to debut at a major tennis event.

Lululemon, best known for its premium athletic and lifestyle apparel, now has governance questions and product development both in the spotlight. The activist effort from its founder puts board structure and long term brand direction under closer scrutiny for shareholders in NasdaqGS:LULU. At the same time, the ShowZero fabric launch highlights the company’s ongoing push to differentiate its products in a crowded performance wear market.

For you as an investor, this combination of boardroom tension and new product launches raises questions about how Lululemon balances creative vision, risk management, and growth priorities. The outcome of the director nominations and market reception to ShowZero may affect how the company allocates capital, invests in product development, and positions the brand with athletes and everyday consumers.

Chip Wilson’s campaign and Lululemon’s ShowZero launch are pulling investor attention in two directions at once. On one side, you have a founder arguing that governance, board skills, and brand focus need a reset, pointing to what he describes as value destruction and weaker creative direction. On the other, management is highlighting product and geographic expansion, from sweat-concealing ShowZero gear co-created with Frances Tiafoe to the 100th store in EMEA and new market entries. For you, the key question is whether the board and future CEO can convert that product pipeline and store growth into durable demand without overextending the brand or balance sheet. Activist pressure can sometimes sharpen execution and accountability, but it can also consume management attention and create uncertainty around long term plans. How management and Wilson engage over the coming months will be an important signal of how aligned the company’s governance, product vision, and capital allocation really are.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The lululemon athletica Narrative

  • Wilson’s focus on creativity and product excellence ties directly to the existing narrative around a reset in product approach and a higher mix of new styles, which is already seen as central to Lululemon’s next phase of growth.
  • The call for governance overhaul and board declassification could challenge the assumption that management can execute its reset without major changes in oversight or potential shifts in priorities under a new CEO.
  • The specific push on board composition, conflicts of interest, and a Brand Product Committee is not fully reflected in the prior narrative, which mainly concentrates on product, margins, tariffs, and international growth rather than shareholder activism.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for lululemon athletica to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Prolonged proxy conflict could distract management from product execution and international expansion plans, especially as a new CEO and creative leadership settle in.
  • ⚠️ Wilson’s criticism of governance and past board decisions highlights the possibility that insider alignment and oversight may not have fully supported the brand’s creative engine, which could weigh on future decision quality if not addressed.
  • 🎁 Lululemon’s strong gross margin, free cash flow profile, and history of product differentiation give it financial flexibility to invest in new fabrics like ShowZero while continuing store openings across EMEA and other regions.
  • 🎁 Activist input, if constructively incorporated, may sharpen focus on creativity, product excellence, and marketing, potentially reinforcing Lululemon’s position against competitors such as Nike, Adidas, and Alo Yoga.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, it is worth watching how the board responds to Wilson’s nominees, whether both sides agree on interviews and engagement, and what governance changes, if any, are proposed before the 2026 annual meeting. On the operating side, pay attention to the sell through and consumer response to ShowZero once it rolls out beyond Tiafoe’s kit, as well as progress on the broader Spring 2026 assortment reset. Store performance in newer EMEA markets, including Poland and other 2026 entries, will also help show whether international expansion is supporting Lululemon’s growth story while activist pressure plays out.

data.title: Wabtec Settlement With Progress Rail Clears Path For Rail Growth

Wabtec, listed as NYSE:WAB, is a major supplier of locomotive equipment and rail technologies, so legal friction with another large supplier has been closely watched by customers and partners. With the dispute now settled, attention can shift back to core themes in freight and passenger rail, such as fleet renewal, digital systems and efficiency upgrades across global rail networks.

For investors, the key takeaway is that a legal overhang tied to a meaningful industry relationship has now been addressed. The settlement may help both companies engage with rail operators and manufacturers with clearer ground rules around technology, contracts and future cooperation or competition.

Both firms explicitly recognize their role in Tier IV long haul locomotives and cab components, so a settlement without any admission of liability can help reduce uncertainty for rail operators that depend on both product sets working together in the same fleets. With litigation off the table, Wabtec can put more focus on contract execution, aftermarket support, and digital add ons, which are all central to its rail equipment and services offering. For you as an investor, the interest is less about the legal details and more about whether management can keep converting its contract pipeline into profitable, long term relationships while organic revenue growth has recently trailed the sector.
